title: Geometric memory in incomplete phase transitions across dimensions
abstract: We model a direct solid-state phase transition through a nucleation-and-growth process in which plates have simple, regular shapes - squares, cubes, or square-faced lamellae - and grow homothetically (self-similarly) until they either reach a randomly assigned maximum size or are stopped by impingement with previously formed plates. The reverse transformation is represented by the preferential disappearance of smaller plates, while larger plates are retained during an incomplete reversion. A subsequent direct transformation therefore produces a modified plate-size distribution, a memory effect that forms the main focus of this study. Building upon an earlier two-dimensional (2D) formulation, we extend the model to cubes (3D) and to lamellar plates (3DL) in order to examine how dimensionality affects transformation memory. We introduce a quantitative descriptor of memory, the size mass ratio, and find that memory is robust in all geometries but overall stronger in 2D than in 3D or 3DL. We provide growth snapshots, arrest-regrowth cycles, size distributions, and differential scanning calorimetry simulations, and we compute the Shannon size-entropy to quantify configurational diversity. Although motivated by the thermal memory effect in shape-memory alloys, the model more generally identifies a purely geometric mechanism for memory in first-order solid-solid transformations, highlighting the role of dimensionality and geometric blocking in controlling the strength of transformation memory.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the memory of phase distributions during incomplete solid-state transitions is driven by a purely geometric mechanism. It shows that the strength of this memory is highly dependent on the dimensionality of the growing phases, being most pronounced in 2D.
main_problem: The study investigates how dimensionality and geometric blocking affect the persistence of memory in the size distribution of phases during incomplete solid-state phase transitions.
main_result: The researchers find that while geometric memory is robust across different geometries, it is significantly stronger in 2D than in 3D or 3D lamellar configurations.
method: The authors use a nucleation-and-growth model involving self-similar expansion and impingement, supplemented by differential scanning calorimetry simulations and Shannon size-entropy calculations.
model_or_system: A model of first-order solid-solid phase transitions where plates (squares, cubes, or lamellae) grow homothetically until they reach a maximum size or are stopped by impingement.
key_observables: Size mass ratio, plate-size distribution, Shannon size-entropy, and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) signals.
important_parameters: Dimensionality (2D, 3D, and 3DL), plate geometry, and the degree of incomplete reversion.
main_assumptions: The phase transition is modeled as a purely geometric process of nucleation and growth with regular plate shapes and self-similar expansion.
figures_summary: The paper includes growth snapshots, arrest-regrowth cycles, size distributions, and simulated DSC curves.
paper_structure: The paper extends a 2D model to 3D and 3DL geometries, introduces a quantitative memory descriptor, analyzes the impact of dimensionality, and provides simulation-based validation.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
